ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fajita class (FOODON_03301710); no prawn-specific fajita subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest parent for prawn-based mixed dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for prawn fajitas as a composite dish. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for prawn fajitas or analogous composite Mexican seafood dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of prawn fajitas consists of two flour tortillas filled with grilled prawns, sautéed bell peppers, and onions, weighing approximately 300 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (USDA SR Legacy #15152) combined with flour tortilla and sautéed vegetables — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for prawn fajitas as a composite dish. Nutrient values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), flour tortilla (FDC ID 325871), and sautéed bell peppers and onions (FDC ID 169967, 170000), weighted to approximate a typical prawn fajita filling ratio of approximately 40% prawn, 30% tortilla, 20% peppers and onions, 10% oil and seasoning. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA Foundation Foods composite dish analogues.

gi_source.title: Tortilla, wheat flour — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GI estimated by weighted carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn fajitas as a composite dish. GI estimated at 50 using a weighted average of the glycemic contributions of the main carbohydrate-bearing components: wheat flour tortilla (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database) and sautéed bell peppers and onions (GI ~15, low-GI vegetables). Prawns contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not affect GI materially. Weighted composite GI calculated as approximately 50, classified as low GI. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (50 × 49.5 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a single serving of prawn fajitas may contribute approximately 55% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-sodium seasoning are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~24.8 g per serving) driven by wheat flour tortillas
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should note that the wheat flour tortillas contribute a moderate glycemic load per serving; substituting with smaller or whole-grain tortillas and increasing the prawn-to-tortilla ratio can help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from prawns (~78 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, prawn fajitas carry moderate concern due to their sodium load and the dietary cholesterol from prawns; however, the low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 fatty acids in prawns are partially favourable, and portion moderation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn fajitas are a Mexican-origin sizzling dish consisting of grilled or sautéed prawns (shrimp) served with colourful bell peppers and onions, wrapped in warm wheat flour tortillas. The dish is seasoned with a smoky fajita spice blend typically containing cumin, chilli powder, garlic, paprika, and lime juice, giving it a bold, tangy, and mildly spicy flavour profile. The prawns provide a firm, slightly springy texture and a sweet, briny taste that contrasts with the soft tortilla and the caramelised sweetness of the cooked peppers and onions. Prawn fajitas are a moderate-energy dish at approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a notable protein contribution of around 10.5 g per 100 g from the crustacean component. The dish contains moderate sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) primarily from the spice blend and seasoning, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low GI), though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate at ~24.8 g due to the tortilla carbohydrate content, which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Prawns are a source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and dietary cholesterol. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains gluten from the wheat tortilla and crustacean allergens. In Singapore, prawn fajitas are served at Mexican and fusion restaurants and represent an adaptation of the Tex-Mex tradition to local seafood preferences.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn fajitas?
Is prawn fajita high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for prawn fajitas as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked shrimp, flour tortilla, and sautéed vegetables in weighted proportions. GI was estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat flour tortilla as the primary carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this composite dish estimation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard ingredient set (prawns, flour tortilla, vegetables, oil). Nutrient values represent a typical restaurant-style prawn fajita serving and may vary depending on the specific recipe, prawn size, tortilla brand, and oil quantity used. The two field survey data restaurants serving this dish (Fiesta Mexico — Three Amigos Indian and Mexican Restaurant; Three Amigos Mexican and Indian — Boat Quay) are noted as source context only and are not used as nutritional evidence. Price range SGD 32.00 preserved from input record. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore. The cuisine tag 'Naan' in the input record is inconsistent with a Mexican dish and has been disregarded as a data artefact from the source platform.
